TranslationContext: This file contains Unit Names for the Empire of Men Faction.

Translations.Comment: Reich = empire/realm. Plural form maintains the grand, multi-kingdom nature. Spanish maintains the plural form emphasizing multiple kingdoms and their imperial scope.
Espadachín conveys skilled swordsman; Imperial emphasizes service to the empire.
De a Pie clearly indicates dismounted/on-foot knights distinguishing from cavalry.
Alabardero is the professional Spanish term for halberd-wielders, maintaining the serious military tone.
Lancero emphasizes pike/lance-based formations typical of imperial armies.
Direct translation emphasizing elite heavy cavalry; the mainstay of imperial power.
Caballería Ligera is the standard term for light cavalry, agile scouts and skirmishers.
Arqueros is the standard term for archers; classic ranged support for formations.
Ballesteros denotes crossbow specialists; more heavily armored and powerful than archers.
Arcabuceros specifically denotes arquebusiers/musketeers; Renaissance-era firearms specialists.
Direct translation; heavy siege artillery that emphasizes imperial firepower.
Mortero is the Spanish equivalent; indirect fire artillery support.
Titán conveys colossal, intimidating power; fitting for massive war machine.
Grifón is the standard Spanish term; majestic heraldic symbol of imperial power.
Dragón commands respect and awe; ultimate aerial weapon of the empire.
Capitán is the universal Spanish military rank; seasoned battle commander.
Comandante de Caballería emphasizes military authority over mounted knights; leader of knightly orders.
Mago emphasizes magical prowess; arcane support serving the imperial cause.
